course.title: RabbitMQ 4.3 Pub/Sub and Reliable Messaging with TypeScript 7
course.description: TypeScript backend developers who can build production HTTP services and need to design, implement, test, operate, and defend asynchronous RabbitMQ systems. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate rabbitmq 4.3.2, amqp 0-9-1, node 24.18, npm 12, typescript 7, amqplib 2.0.1, architecture selection, exchanges, queues, bindings, connections, channels, heartbeats, recovery, topology, direct and fanout pub/sub, topic and headers routing, envelopes, asyncapi 3.1, cloudevents 1.0.2, publisher backpressure, returns, confirms, consumers, acknowledgements, prefetch, idempotency, retries, dead lettering, quorum queues, rabbitmq 4.3 delayed retry and priorities, ordering, streams, outbox and sagas, tls, authorization, opentelemetry 1.43, prometheus, capacity, testing, upgrades, and recovery through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate runnable deterministic pure-typescript messaging decision functions plus explicit clean compilation and disposable rabbitmq, tls, partition, restart, load, upgrade, and production transfer gates through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
Demonstrate five cumulative messaging systems and a performance-based production defense exam through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves TypeScript, HTTP service, accessibility, security, validation, persistence, testing, lifecycle, and evidence habits before adding one messaging boundary.

Browser TypeScript is deterministic and isolated. It models topology, routing, delivery, acknowledgement, retry, and recovery decisions but never imports amqplib or Node built-ins, opens sockets, contacts RabbitMQ, Docker, networks, management APIs, databases, or external effects, reads host state, or uses credentials.

Passing work requires stable message and scenario identity, a named ownership transfer, bounded bytes, time, attempts, and concurrency, observable changed and failure results, cleanup and recovery, and explicit disposable-broker or production transfer limits.

credential.title: RabbitMQ 4.3 Pub/Sub and Reliable Messaging with TypeScript 7 Certificate

modules.title: Messaging Outcomes, Delivery Claims, and Evidence
modules.description: A team calls every background task reliable pub/sub, promises exactly-once delivery, and reports publish calls instead of stakeholder, ownership, loss, duplicate, latency, and recovery evidence. Learners cumulatively produce messaging outcome, delivery claim, and evidence charter through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Define producer, consumer, operator, security, accessibility, latency, loss, duplicate, and recovery evidence for one messaging outcome.
Distinguish commands, events, notifications, documents, and queries by ownership, audience, expected handling, and change semantics.
State at-most-once, at-least-once, effectively-once effect, ordering, and durability claims without promising impossible end-to-end exactly-once delivery.

modules.title: Asynchronous Architecture and Pattern Selection
modules.description: A request path adds a broker without a latency or failure reason, hides synchronous coupling behind RPC messages, and uses one global queue for work distribution, broadcasting, and replay. Learners cumulatively produce pattern selection and coupling decision record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Choose asynchronous messaging only when temporal decoupling, buffering, fan-out, failure isolation, or replay creates measurable value.
Distinguish competing consumers on one queue from publish/subscribe using one queue per subscriber responsibility.
Evaluate request/reply over messaging against ordinary HTTP using correlation, timeout, cancellation, failure ambiguity, and capacity evidence.

modules.title: AMQP 0-9-1 Model, Virtual Hosts, Exchanges, Queues, and Bindings
modules.description: A diagram sends publishers directly to shared queues, confuses exchanges with storage, ignores virtual-host isolation, and cannot explain how routing keys and bindings produce zero, one, or many destinations. Learners cumulatively produce AMQP entity and routing graph through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace producers, connections, channels, exchanges, bindings, queues, consumers, deliveries, and acknowledgements without collapsing their roles.
Use virtual hosts as separate topology and permission namespaces while recognizing they are not complete process or resource isolation.
Derive destination sets from exchange type, routing key, binding key, headers, and exchange-to-exchange bindings.

modules.title: Node 24, TypeScript 7, and amqplib 2.0.1 Contract
modules.description: A service mixes callback and promise APIs, assumes publish returns a Promise, trusts TypeScript types as runtime validation, and exits before asynchronous close and confirms settle. Learners cumulatively produce typed amqplib lifecycle and boundary contract through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Pin Node 24.18, npm 12, TypeScript 7, amqplib 2.0.1, AMQP 0-9-1, and RabbitMQ 4.3.2 as one reviewed compatibility contract.
Choose one amqplib promise or callback API and account for RPC methods, event emitters, callback-only confirms, and non-RPC methods.
Treat delivered bytes, fields, properties, headers, and decoded payload as untrusted runtime data despite TypeScript declarations.

modules.title: Connections, Channels, Heartbeats, Errors, and Recovery
modules.description: A service opens one TCP connection per message, shares one channel across unrelated concurrent owners, disables heartbeats, retries instantly, and redeclares consumers before topology or credentials recover. Learners cumulatively produce connection, channel, heartbeat, and recovery state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use bounded long-lived AMQP connections and explain multiplexed channels, negotiated limits, names, TLS, and lifecycle cost.
Assign channel ownership by publisher or consumer lifecycle and prevent concurrent confirm, acknowledgement, and topology operations from corrupting assumptions.
Choose a heartbeat timeout with network and flow-control evidence and distinguish heartbeat interval, TCP keepalive, and false-positive failure.

modules.title: Topology Declarations, Property Equivalence, Policies, and Ownership
modules.description: Multiple services declare the same queue with different durability and arguments, hardcode every operational x-argument, delete shared topology at startup, and turn precondition failures into reconnect loops. Learners cumulatively produce versioned topology declaration and ownership manifest through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Assign ownership for exchanges, queues, bindings, policies, migrations, rollback, and deletion across application and platform teams.
Make repeated declarations equivalent in type, durability, exclusivity, auto-delete, and immutable arguments and diagnose precondition mismatch.
Choose durable, exclusive server-named, auto-delete, and queue-TTL lifecycles from responsibility and race evidence.

modules.title: Default and Direct Exchanges, Work Queues, and Fair Dispatch
modules.description: A task service publishes through the default exchange without naming the coupling, assumes one queue broadcasts, uses auto-ack workers, and calls round-robin dispatch fair despite unequal task cost. Learners cumulatively produce direct-routing work distribution service through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Explain the default exchange queue-name binding and choose it only when direct queue-name coupling is intentional.
Design direct exchange bindings that keep producer intent stable while consumer queues evolve.
Use one queue with multiple consumers for load distribution and state that each delivery goes to one active consumer at a time.

modules.title: Fanout Exchanges and True Publish/Subscribe
modules.description: Audit, notification, search, and analytics consumers share one queue and steal work from each other; a fanout exchange exists but late or disconnected subscriber behavior is undocumented. Learners cumulatively produce fanout subscription topology and lifecycle map through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Give each independent subscriber responsibility its own queue so every responsibility can receive and acknowledge its copy.
Use fanout routing when every binding should match and record the resulting copy count and storage responsibility.
Design exclusive server-named subscriptions for session-scoped state and account for disconnect, binding, declaration, and cleanup races.

modules.title: Topic, Headers, Alternate, and Exchange-to-Exchange Routing
modules.description: Routing keys encode unstable JSON, topic wildcards overlap unexpectedly, headers matching trusts absent fields, and unroutable messages vanish because mandatory and alternate behavior are not designed together. Learners cumulatively produce multi-strategy routing and unroutable-message table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Design bounded dot-separated topic taxonomies whose stable semantic segments support exact, star, and hash matching.
Enumerate overlapping topic bindings and prove which queues receive one copy even when multiple bindings on the same queue match.
Use headers exchanges only with typed bounded metadata and explicit all-versus-any matching, not as a schema-free routing escape hatch.

modules.title: Message Envelopes, Binary Bodies, Schemas, and Evolution
modules.description: Consumers parse Buffer content directly into trusted domain types, overload routing keys with identity data, lack stable event IDs and versions, and break when producers add or remove fields. Learners cumulatively produce versioned message envelope and compatibility matrix through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Bound message bytes, verify content type and encoding, decode once, parse safely, and keep malformed content out of domain effects.
Define message ID, type, schema version, source, subject, occurrence time, correlation, causation, tenant, and trace context without conflating them.
Validate unknown envelopes and payloads with strict runtime schemas, bounded collections, semantic refinements, and explicit rejection evidence.

modules.title: Publisher Buffers, Drain, Admission, and Bounded Batching
modules.description: A publisher awaits a boolean from publish, ignores false, buffers unbounded work during broker flow control, and batches until memory exhaustion while upstream requests continue to receive success. Learners cumulatively produce publisher admission and buffer-pressure controller through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Treat amqplib publish and sendToQueue return values as writable-buffer admission signals rather than delivery confirmations or Promises.
Pause admission after false, coordinate a bounded drain waiter, handle close and abort, and avoid lost wakeups or listener leaks.
Propagate publisher capacity to HTTP or job admission with queue, byte, time, tenant, and rejection budgets.

modules.title: Mandatory Publishing, Returns, Alternate Exchanges, and Route Audits
modules.description: The service publishes with mandatory false, treats confirms as proof of routing, attaches the return listener after publishing, and retries unroutable messages forever without fixing topology. Learners cumulatively produce unroutable publication detection and ownership record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Register return handling before mandatory publication and correlate returned content, exchange, routing key, properties, and publisher intent.
Distinguish broker confirms from consumer processing and combine mandatory returns or alternate routing with confirm results.
Build topology fixtures that assert expected destination sets, forbidden routes, unroutable cases, permissions, and changed bindings.

modules.title: Publisher Confirms, Windows, Failure Ambiguity, and Republish
modules.description: A producer opens a normal channel, calls waitForConfirms after every message, discards sequence identity, and blindly republishes all work after a connection loss even when the broker may already own it. Learners cumulatively produce confirm-window and ambiguous-publication ledger through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use confirm channels and account for callback results, waitForConfirms, nack, return ordering, close, and unresolved publication state.
Bound unconfirmed count, bytes, and age while preserving throughput, memory, fairness, and per-message outcome evidence.
Explain when confirms occur for unroutable messages, persistent classic queues, quorum queues, and multiple routed queues.

modules.title: Consumer Lifecycle, Cancellation, Readiness, and Graceful Shutdown
modules.description: A consumer reports ready before consume-ok, ignores broker cancellation, closes the connection while handlers run, and assumes cancel immediately stops every in-flight delivery. Learners cumulatively produce consumer registration, readiness, and shutdown state machine through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Report ready only after connection, topology, channel, prefetch, consume registration, and required dependencies are ready.
Track consumer tags and channel generations so stale cancellation, delivery, and close events cannot mutate current state.
Handle a null delivery callback and cancellation notifications as lifecycle events with cause, readiness, recovery, and ownership evidence.

modules.title: Acknowledgements, Delivery Tags, Redelivery, and Effect Ownership
modules.description: A handler acknowledges on a different channel, auto-acks before validation, nacks every error with requeue, treats redelivered false as first-ever processing, and double-acks after a timeout race. Learners cumulatively produce delivery ownership and acknowledgement decision table through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Choose manual or automatic acknowledgement from loss tolerance, consumer memory, work duration, and effect ownership.
Acknowledge, nack, or reject a delivery exactly once on its owning channel and prevent stale or cross-channel tag use.
Transfer ownership only after the intended durable effect, transactional inbox record, or safely repeatable handoff succeeds.

modules.title: Prefetch, Consumer Concurrency, Capacity, and Fairness
modules.description: A consumer sets prefetch zero for maximum speed, starts unbounded promises, ignores message bytes and dependency pools, and scales consumers from queue depth alone. Learners cumulatively produce prefetch, concurrency, and capacity budget model through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Explain RabbitMQ per-consumer prefetch, deprecated global QoS, in-flight races, quorum caps, and the zero-means-unbounded hazard.
Bound concurrent handlers by CPU, memory, message bytes, downstream pools, deadlines, tenant fairness, and shutdown capacity.
Interpret consumer capacity with delivery rate, processing latency, ready and unacked depth, dependency saturation, and queue single-core limits.

modules.title: Idempotent Consumers, Inbox Records, and External Effects
modules.description: A consumer uses an in-memory set, marks messages complete before effects, reuses IDs across tenants, and assumes an idempotency key makes non-transactional email and payment calls exactly once. Learners cumulatively produce durable inbox and duplicate-safe effect protocol through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Derive a stable scoped operation identity from producer intent, tenant, message type, and semantic version rather than delivery metadata.
Record inbox identity, input fingerprint, state transition, domain effect, and completion atomically in one database transaction where possible.
Reject reuse of an operation identity with a different payload fingerprint or semantic intent and retain conflict evidence.

modules.title: Failure Classification, Retry Budgets, Backoff, and Load Shedding
modules.description: Every exception is requeued immediately, permanent schema failures block healthy work, a database outage causes a retry storm, and attempt counts exist only in process memory. Learners cumulatively produce failure taxonomy and bounded retry policy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Classify malformed, unauthorized, missing dependency, rate limit, timeout, conflict, transient, permanent, and ambiguous effect failures by evidence.
Bound attempts, total age, delay, processing deadline, queue residence, and operator escalation as one retry budget.
Apply exponential backoff and jitter without blocking consumer slots or requeueing hot loops and preserve intended availability time.

modules.title: Dead Lettering, Poison Messages, Delivery Limits, and Parking
modules.description: A queue hardcodes a missing DLX, cycles messages indefinitely, drops quorum poison messages after the default delivery limit, and treats a dead-letter queue as an unowned archive. Learners cumulatively produce dead-letter, poison, and remediation topology through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Trace nack or reject without requeue, expiry, length overflow, and quorum delivery-limit dead lettering through exchange and routing policy.
Configure dead lettering through mutable policy where possible and verify source read, target write, exchange existence, binding, and route permissions.
Detect and bound dead-letter cycles using history, stable identity, attempt policy, route analysis, and a terminal parking destination.

modules.title: Quorum Queues, Raft Replication, Availability, and Data Safety
modules.description: A team declares quorum queues for ephemeral sessions, assumes any replica count survives any failure, omits confirms and manual acknowledgements, and ignores leader locality, majority loss, backlog, and upgrade behavior. Learners cumulatively produce quorum queue safety and availability decision record through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Choose quorum queues for durable replicated data safety and reject them for high-churn temporary queues, lowest latency, or extreme backlogs better served by streams.
Derive tolerated failures and availability from odd replica count, online majority, leader election, placement, and maintenance sequence.
Combine persistent messages, publisher confirms, manual consumer acknowledgements, and duplicate-safe applications for quorum data safety.

modules.title: RabbitMQ 4.3 Delayed Retries, Priorities, and Consumer Timeouts
modules.description: A design copies old TTL dead-letter retry loops into RabbitMQ 4.3, uses priority as unlimited preemption, leaves consumer timeouts unrelated to handler deadlines, and assumes new broker behavior exists on older clusters. Learners cumulatively produce RabbitMQ 4.3 feature and fallback compatibility plan through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use RabbitMQ 4.3 quorum delayed-retry policies for returned or failed deliveries with explicit delay, eligibility, attempt, and compatibility evidence.
Compare native delayed retries with TTL and DLX cycles by rewrite cost, loss and duplicate windows, head-of-line effects, topology, and version support.
Use RabbitMQ 4.3 quorum 32 strict priority levels with bounded use, starvation evidence, prefetch interaction, and per-priority monitoring.

modules.title: Ordering, Single Active Consumer, Keys, and Partitioning
modules.description: A service promises global order across publishers and channels, adds parallel consumers, requeues failures, uses priority, then depends on exact FIFO completion for each customer. Learners cumulatively produce ordering scope and partition strategy through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: State ordering scope across producer channel, exchange route, one queue, delivery, completion, redelivery, priority, and multiple consumers.
Partition related messages by stable business key while bounding skew, hot keys, resharding, and cross-key independence.
Use single active consumer when one active delivery stream is required and account for failover, prefetch, recovery, and throughput limits.

modules.title: Queues, Streams, Super Streams, Retention, and Replay
modules.description: A high-throughput replay workload uses one quorum queue with a ten-million-message backlog, while another service selects streams but expects destructive consumption and ordinary queue client locality. Learners cumulatively produce queue-versus-stream workload decision and replay plan through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Compare destructive queue consumption with retained stream offsets, replay, retention, protocol clients, and consumer responsibility.
Define age and byte retention, segment behavior, start offset, consumer progress, replay authorization, and late-reader expectations.
Use super streams to partition throughput by key while accounting for partitions, routing, per-partition order, skew, and consumer coordination.

modules.title: Transactional Outbox, Relay, Inbox, Sagas, and Compensation
modules.description: An HTTP transaction commits database state then publishes, or publishes then rolls back; a relay deletes outbox rows before confirms; a multi-service workflow treats retries as rollback. Learners cumulatively produce outbox, relay, inbox, and compensation protocol through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Enumerate database-first, publish-first, timeout, crash, confirm-loss, rollback, and duplicate failure windows for a dual write.
Store domain mutation and immutable outbox message identity, type, payload, destination intent, and availability atomically.
Claim bounded outbox rows, publish with backpressure and confirms, mark completion safely, and recover ambiguous attempts through stable identity.

modules.title: TLS, Authentication, Virtual Hosts, Authorization, and Secrets
modules.description: A production client uses amqp with guest credentials, grants configure/write/read wildcards across the default virtual host, logs connection URLs, disables certificate verification, and cannot rotate credentials without downtime. Learners cumulatively produce messaging identity, transport, and least-privilege matrix through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Use TLS with trusted CA, hostname and SNI verification, minimum protocol policy, client identity where required, and tested certificate rotation.
Choose generated credentials, x509 EXTERNAL, or OAuth 2 identity with explicit broker, client, token, renewal, and revocation support.
Grant minimum configure, write, and read regex permissions per virtual host and separately evaluate topic authorization.

modules.title: Observability, Capacity, Flow Control, Alarms, and SLOs
modules.description: A dashboard shows only queue depth, creates message-ID metric labels, ignores unacknowledged work and oldest age, and responds to memory alarms by adding publishers and increasing prefetch. Learners cumulatively produce messaging telemetry, capacity, and SLO evidence board through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Instrument publish, confirm, return, route, deliver, process, ack, retry, dead-letter, and effect phases with bounded identities and causal links.
Interpret ready, unacknowledged, total, ingress, delivery, ack, redelivery, consumers, consumer capacity, connections, channels, and node resource metrics together.
Model arrival rate, service rate, concurrency, oldest age, burst, retry amplification, byte volume, and recovery time for capacity decisions.

modules.title: Contract Tests, Fault Injection, Deployment, Upgrade, and Recovery
modules.description: Tests mock amqplib method calls but never assert routing or failure behavior; deployment starts consumers before topology migration; a reconnect storm follows broker restart; rollback cannot read new message schemas. Learners cumulatively produce tested, recoverable RabbitMQ production release through theory, guided work, debugging, transfer, retrieval, and assessment.
objectives: Test pure policy, envelope schema, topology routes, publisher confirms, consumer effects, disposable broker integration, and production probes at distinct layers.
Inject return, nack, close, heartbeat loss, blocked connection, duplicate, poison, dependency timeout, majority loss, restart, and stale callback failures deterministically.
Roll out schema, topology, publisher, consumer, policy, and broker changes in a compatibility-safe order with shadow evidence and rollback.
